---
title: "Data"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(readxl)
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)

# Load raw data
r8    <- read_excel("case-and-demographic-characteristics-table-r8-2023-2024.xlsx",
                    col_names = FALSE)
r100  <- read_excel("case-and-demographic-characteristics-table-r100-2023-2024.xlsx",
                    col_names = FALSE)
fatal <- read_excel("fatal.xlsx", col_names = FALSE)

# --- Nonfatal rates from R8 ---
r8_data <- r8[-c(1, 2), ]
names(r8_data)[1]  <- "industry"
names(r8_data)[2]  <- "naics"
names(r8_data)[3]  <- "case_type"
names(r8_data)[4]  <- "total_rate"
names(r8_data)[10] <- "fall_rate"

mfg <- r8_data |> filter(naics == "31-33", case_type == "DAFW")
mfg_total <- as.numeric(mfg$total_rate[1])  # 86.4
mfg_falls <- as.numeric(mfg$fall_rate[1])   # 18.7

fin <- r8_data |> filter(naics == "52", case_type == "DAFW")
fin_total <- as.numeric(fin$total_rate[1])   # 11.0
fin_falls <- as.numeric(fin$fall_rate[1])    # 3.7

pro <- r8_data |> filter(naics == "54", case_type == "DAFW")
pro_total <- as.numeric(pro$total_rate[1])   # 18.6
pro_falls <- as.numeric(pro$fall_rate[1])    # 4.3

# Fall share of total injuries (nonfatal)
mfg_fall_share <- round(mfg_falls / mfg_total * 100, 1)
fin_fall_share <- round(fin_falls / fin_total * 100, 1)
pro_fall_share <- round(pro_falls / pro_total * 100, 1)

# --- Fatal injury data (hardcoded from CFOI Table A-1) ---
mfg_fatal_total <- 353
mfg_fatal_falls <- 52

fin_fatal_total <- 18
fin_fatal_falls <- 6

pro_fatal_total <- 65
pro_fatal_falls <- 10

mfg_fall_pct <- round(mfg_fatal_falls / mfg_fatal_total * 100, 1)
fin_fall_pct <- round(fin_fatal_falls / fin_fatal_total * 100, 1)
pro_fall_pct <- round(pro_fatal_falls / pro_fatal_total * 100, 1)
```


## Data

### Nonfatal Injuries: SOII

The Survey of Occupational Injuries and Illnesses (SOII) is published annually by the BLS, as required by the Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970 [@bls_soii_tables]. It surveys approximately 200,000 employers and reports injury rates per 10,000 full-time equivalent (FTE) workers. The most recent release covers 2023–2024 [@bls_soii_2024].

For this report, we used Table R8, which breaks down nonfatal injury rates by industry and event type (including falls, slips, and trips). We focused on Days Away From Work (DAFW) rates, which capture injuries serious enough to keep workers out of work.

### Fatal Injuries: CFOI

The Census of Fatal Occupational Injuries (CFOI) is an annual count of every workplace death in the United States [@bls_cfoi_tables]. Unlike the SOII, which is a sample survey, the CFOI verifies each case using multiple independent sources such as death certificates, OSHA reports, and workers' compensation records. The most recent release covers 2024 [@bls_cfoi_2024].

We used Table A-1, which reports fatal injury counts by industry and event type, including falls.
